articles.title: New international student permit approvals for Canada fell below COVID levels in 2025
articles.summary: Canada approved 75,372 new study permits for international students in 2025. This figure represents a 64% year-over-year decline and an 18% decrease from the 2020 pandemic low.
articles.insight:
Canada’s sharp 2025 study permit decline—falling below even pandemic-era lows—may reflect tightened immigration policies, reduced post-study work incentives, or shifting perceptions among key source markets, though it doesn’t directly confirm broader global demand shifts.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application trends from overlapping source countries (e.g., India, Nigeria, Philippines), monitor policy announcements in Canada and peer destinations, and assess whether competitor institutions are adjusting messaging around affordability, pathways, or settlement support.

articles.title: UK: 7 in 10 universities report declining international postgraduate enrolments; visa rejections are part of the story
articles.summary: A recent survey by the British Universities International Liaison Association (BUILA) found that 70% of UK universities reported a decline in international postgraduate enrolments in January 2026 compared with January 2025, with an average reduction of 31%. The decline was particularly pronounced among students from “high-risk” markets, and visa rejections were cited as a contributing factor.
articles.insight:
This UK enrolment decline—driven partly by visa rejections and concentrated in high-risk markets—may signal growing sensitivity among international postgraduates to immigration policy uncertainty, though it’s unclear whether this reflects broader demand shifts or UK-specific friction.  
Admissions teams should monitor application trends from overlapping source markets, track peer institutions’ visa support services and pre-arrival guidance, and assess whether their own visa refusal rates or student support pathways warrant review amid tightening global immigration scrutiny.

articles.title: Number of Hong Kong school leavers seeking education elsewhere hits 14-year low
articles.summary: The number of Hong Kong secondary school leavers pursuing higher education outside the city dropped to 2,671 in 2025, representing 6.5% of the 40,948 Form Six graduates enrolled in full-time courses, marking a 13% decline from 2024’s figure of 3,061 and the lowest level in 14 years. An expert cited increased competition from mainland Chinese students applying to local universities as a key factor behind this trend.
articles.insight:
Hong Kong’s sharp 13% drop in outbound secondary leavers—reaching a 14-year low—may reflect shifting preferences toward local or mainland Chinese universities, though it’s unclear whether this signals broader regional demand softening or is driven by specific policy, cost, or perception factors affecting only this cohort.  
Admissions teams should monitor Hong Kong’s Form Six enrolment trends alongside visa approval rates and sentiment surveys, while assessing whether mainland Chinese competition for local places is indirectly influencing perceptions of overseas study value—not as an immediate red flag, but as a contextual factor in market positioning.
